1: qərāʾ nāʾ hayēš ʿōnekā wəʾel mî miqqəḏōšîm tippāneh: 'Call now; is there anyone who will answer you? To which of the holy ones will you turn?' Eliphaz's rhetorical question deploys the divine council motif: the qəḏōšîm (holy ones) are the heavenly beings who surround YHWH's throne (Ps 89:5, 7; Zech 14:5). His point is that Job has no one in the divine court to intercede for him — a bitter irony since the reader knows Job's case has already been argued before YHWH's council in the prologue. The rhetorical device silences complaint by claiming the heavenly court is closed to the sufferer. Job's longing for an arbiter (9:33) is the direct counterpoint to this dismissal.
2: kî-ləʾəwîl yaharōg-kaʿaś ûpōṯeh tāmîṯ qinʾāh: 'For vexation kills the fool, and jealousy slays the simple.' Eliphaz here introduces his retribution framework: emotional excess (kaʿaś, vexation/anger, and qinʾāh, jealousy/zeal) is the mark of the ʾěwîl (the fool who lacks moral discernment). The grammatical subject of the killing is the emotions themselves — vexation is self-destructive. This functions as a veiled accusation: Job's bitter complaint in ch3 is evidence of the very fool-anger that brings ruin. The lexical pair ʾěwîl / pōṯeh (fool / simple) appears together in Proverbs (e.g., Prov 7:7; 22:3) as the two grades of moral deficiency.
3: ʾănî-rāʾîṯî ʾěwîl mašrîš wayyiqqōḇ nāwêhû pîṯʾōm: 'I have seen the fool taking root, but suddenly I cursed his dwelling.' The verb mašrîš (taking root, hiphil of šāraš) depicts the apparent stability of the wicked — a Psalm-like image of the wicked flourishing temporarily (cf. Ps 37:35). But pîṯʾōm (suddenly) marks the reversal: the dwelling (nāweh) is cursed. Eliphaz presents this as personal experience (ʾănî rāʾîṯî, I myself have seen), grounding retribution theology in empirical observation rather than divine revelation. Job's speeches will contest precisely this claim to empirical adequacy.
4: yirḥăqû bānāyw mîyešaʿ wəyiddakkəʾû ḇaššaʿar wəʾên maṣṣîl: 'His children are far from safety, crushed in the gate, with none to deliver.' The shaʿar (gate) was the site of legal proceedings in ancient Israel: the gate community could vindicate or condemn. To be dāḵaʾ (crushed, beaten down) in the gate is to be legally destroyed without advocate. This resonates brutally with Job's situation: his own children died (1:18-19), and no one interceded. Yet Eliphaz is applying it to the progeny of a hypothetical ʾěwîl — the audience (and reader) cannot miss the implication that Job's children died because of their father's sin. This is precisely the retributive calculus YHWH will condemn in 42:7.
5: ʾăšer qəṣîrô rāʿēḇ yōʾḵēl wəʾel-miṣṣinnîm yiqqāḥēhû wəšāʾap̄ ṣammîm ḥêlām: 'The hungry eat his harvest, even from behind thorns, and the thirsty pant after his wealth.' The verse depicts the complete unraveling of the wicked man's household economy — harvest, property, and water all stripped away by predators. The phrase miṣṣinnîm (from behind thorns/a thorn-hedge) suggests even his protective hedgerow is breached. The term ṣammîm is rare and disputed (some translate 'thirsty ones,' others 'snares'); the sense of comprehensive plunder is clear. This economic imagery for divine judgment is conventional in wisdom literature (Prov 13:22; 28:8).
6: kî lōʾ-yēṣēʾ meʿāp̄ār ʾāwen wûmēʾăḏāmāh lōʾ-yiṣmaḥ ʿāmāl: 'For affliction does not come from the dust, nor does trouble sprout from the ground.' Eliphaz makes a causal claim: suffering (ʾāwen, trouble/wickedness, and ʿāmāl, toil/misery) is not a random natural phenomenon like weeds springing from the ground. The verse prepares for v.7's counter-assertion. The implicit syllogism: suffering has a cause → causes are moral → therefore suffering implies sin. The verse is theologically sophisticated but logically flawed in Job's case: it confuses 'suffering has a cause' with 'the cause is always the sufferer's sin.'
7: kî-ʾāḏām leyāʾmāl yûllāḏ ûḇənê-rešep̄ yagbîhû ʿûp̄: 'For man is born to trouble as the bənê-rešep̄ fly upward.' The phrase bənê-rešep̄ (sons of Resheph) is one of Job's most contested idioms. Resheph was the Canaanite deity of plague and fire (cf. Ps 78:48 where rešep̄ = bolts of fire/lightning). Bənê-rešep̄ thus means either 'sparks' (the visual image of sparks flying upward from a fire) or 'birds of prey' (some ancient versions). The majority view is sparks: just as fire inevitably produces sparks that fly upward, man inevitably produces trouble (ʿāmāl). The verse supports Eliphaz's retribution logic: trouble is as natural a product of human existence as sparks from fire — implying Job's situation is simply the universal lot made visible. Eliphaz intends this as comfort, but Job experiences it as cold.
8: ʾûlām ʾănî ʾeḏrōš ʾel-ʾēl wəʾel-ʾělōhîm ʾāśîm diḇrāṯî: 'As for me, I would seek God, and to God I would commit my cause.' Eliphaz now pivots to positive exhortation: the solution to Job's situation is not complaint but petition. The verb dāraš (seek, inquire) has cultic overtones in the OT (seeking YHWH at his sanctuary, consulting an oracle). ʾĒl and ʾělōhîm are used in parallelism here, as they often are in Job (the divine names throughout Job's poetic sections reflect its international/wisdom setting rather than the covenant YHWH-name). The irony: Job is doing exactly what Eliphaz prescribes (seeking God with his complaint), but Eliphaz cannot recognize Job's lament as legitimate petition.
9: ʿōśeh gəḏōlōṯ wəʾên ḥēqer nip̄lāʾōṯ ʿaḏ-ʾên mispar: 'who does great things and unsearchable, marvelous things without number.' The hymnic participles ʿōśeh... nip̄lāʾōṯ (doing great things... marvelous things) open a doxological section (vv.9-16) that celebrates God's sovereign control of creation and history. Nip̄lāʾōṯ (wonders, marvelous deeds) is the standard vocabulary for divine salvific actions in the Psalms (e.g., Ps 9:1; 26:7; 40:5). The participial construction identifies who God is by what he continuously does. The same vocabulary will appear in YHWH's whirlwind speech (38-41), where creation's incomprehensibility vindicates divine wisdom over human theodicy.
10: hannōṯēn māṭār ʿal-pənê-ʾāreṣ wəšōlēaḥ mayim ʿal-pənê ḥûṣôṯ: 'who gives rain on the earth and sends waters on the fields.' The participle chain continues: God's providential supply of rain is cited as evidence of his great deeds. In ancient Near Eastern thought, rain was the prerogative of the storm deity (Baal, in Canaanite religion); here it is attributed to ʾĒl/ʾělōhîm. This verse functions as a polemic: the God who controls rain is the God who controls all life — the same God who controls Job's circumstances. Eliphaz's intent is consolatory (this great God can restore you), but the verse also sets up YHWH's own interrogation of Job: 'Can you send forth lightnings ... do you know the ordinances of heaven and can you establish their rule on the earth?' (38:35, 33).
11: lāśûm šəp̄ālîm ləmārôm wəqōḏərîm śāgəbû yešaʿ: 'to set on high those who are lowly, and those who mourn are lifted to safety.' The reversal of fortunes motif (šəp̄ālîm = low ones raised; qōḏərîm = those in mourning/dressed in dark sackcloth, lifted) is a cornerstone of biblical theology of divine justice (cf. 1 Sam 2:7-8 in Hannah's song; Luke 1:52 in the Magnificat). YHWH characteristically reverses social stratifications — the proud brought low, the humble exalted. Eliphaz uses this to suggest that if Job humbles himself (submits to discipline), God will restore him. This theological point is not wrong in itself — the error is the application to Job's specific case as a formula.
12: mēp̄êr maḥšəḇôṯ ʿărûmîm wəlōʾ-taʿăśênāh yəḏêhem tûšiyyāh: 'He frustrates the devices of the crafty, so that their hands achieve no success.' The vocabulary here is wisdom-register: ʿărûmîm (crafty/shrewd, the same word as the serpent in Gen 3:1) and tûšiyyāh (abiding success/sound wisdom, a wisdom-literature term, appearing also in Job 11:6; 12:16; Prov 2:7; 3:21; 8:14). God is the one who unravels the schemes of those who use cleverness without reverence. This is the wisdom teaching that human strategy ultimately cannot prevail against divine sovereignty — a point Paul cites in 1 Cor 3:19 from v.13.
13: lōkēḏ ḥăkāmîm bəʿormāṯām wăʿăṣaṯ nip̄tālîm niməhārāh: 'He catches the wise in their own craftiness, and the schemes of the wily are brought to a quick end.' ʿormāh (craftiness) is the instrument of their own entrapment: divine irony uses the wicked's own cleverness as the net. The hapax nip̄tālîm (twisted/wily ones, from pāṯal = to twist, wrestle) appears in Gen 30:8 of Jacob's wrestling and in Prov 8:8 of perverse speech. Paul quotes v.13 in 1 Cor 3:19 ('He catches the wise in their craftiness') as part of his argument that worldly wisdom is foolishness before God — a direct citation that establishes Paul's awareness of the Job context and its anti-sophistry thrust.
14: yômām yip̄gəšû-ḥōšeḵ ûḵallaylāh yəmašəšû ḇaṣṣohărāyim: 'They meet with darkness in the daytime and grope at noonday as in the night.' The inversion of light/dark (day becomes darkness, noon becomes night-groping) echoes the Deuteronomic covenant curse: 'You shall grope at noonday, as the blind grope in darkness' (Deut 28:29). This is covenant-curse language applied to the crafty enemies of God — a bold claim that the wicked are experiencing the darkness of divine judgment even in their apparent success. The image of groping in darkness will recur in Job 12:25 where Job applies it to YHWH's sovereign confusion of the mighty.
15: wayyōšaʿ mēḥereḇ mippîhem ûmiyadꞌ ḥāzāq ʾeḇyôn: 'But he saves the needy from the sword of their mouth and from the hand of the mighty.' The transition from the wicked being frustrated (vv.12-14) to the needy being saved completes the reversal structure: God confounds the powerful and delivers the powerless. ʾeḇyôn (the needy/destitute) is the standard vocabulary in the Psalms and prophets for the socially vulnerable who depend entirely on divine justice (Ps 9:18; 12:5; 35:10; Amos 2:6; Isa 25:4). The ḥereḇ pîhem (sword of their mouth) anticipates the NT's metaphor of the word as a sword (Heb 4:12; Rev 1:16).
16: wattəhî laddal tiqwāh wəʿolātāh qāp̄əṣāh-pîhā: 'So the poor have hope, and injustice shuts her mouth.' The verse summarizes the theodicy argument of vv.11-16: divine action on behalf of the lowly generates hope (tiqwāh) for the dal (poor/weak) and silences ʿolātāh (injustice, wrongdoing — from ʿāwal, to be crooked/unjust). Injustice is personified as a speaker whose mouth is shut — a courtroom image. The hope of the poor is a crucial OT theological motif (cf. Ps 9:18: 'For the needy shall not always be forgotten, and the hope of the poor shall not perish forever'). Eliphaz uses this as comfort for Job, assuming Job is among the poor who will be vindicated — if he submits.
17: hinnēh ʾašrê ʾěnôš yôkiḥennû ʾělôah ûmûsar šadday ʾal-timʾās: 'Behold, blessed is the one whom God reproves; therefore despise not the discipline of the Almighty.' The ʾašrê formula (beatitude: blessed/happy is...) is the opening of Psalm 1 and a key wisdom genre marker. Applied here to the one who receives divine reproof (yāḵaḥ), it presents suffering-as-discipline as a mark of divine favor. Šadday (the Almighty, El Shaddai) is the primary divine name in Job's poetic sections (31 of the 48 OT occurrences are in Job) — likely reflecting the wisdom literature's use of a more universal divine name appropriate to a non-Israelite setting. Hebrews 12:5-6 quotes this verse directly: 'My son, do not regard lightly the discipline of the Lord, nor be weary when reproved by him. For the Lord disciplines the one he loves.' The NT citation transforms the verse from retribution comfort into positive theology of sonship.
18: kî hûʾ yakʾîḇ wəyeḥbāš yimḥaṣ wəyādāyw tirp̄ênāh: 'For he wounds, but he binds up; he shatters, but his hands heal.' The divine antithesis (wound/bind, shatter/heal) is a foundational OT theological pattern: YHWH kills and makes alive (Deut 32:39; 1 Sam 2:6); he brings down to Sheol and raises up. The verbs ḵāʾaḇ (to be in pain, cause pain) and ḥāḇaš (to bind up, as a wound) describe the same medical action — infliction followed by treatment. The theological claim is that divine judgment and divine restoration come from the same hands: suffering is not evidence of abandonment but of active divine engagement. This verse underpins the book's theodicy: the hands that wound are the hands that will bind — but the timing is God's, not ours.
19: ḇəšēš ṣārôṯ yaṣṣîleḵā ûḇəšeḇaʿ lōʾ-yiggaʿ bəḵā rāʿ: 'He will deliver you from six troubles; in seven no evil shall touch you.' The numerical saying (x, x+1) is a classic wisdom literary form (cf. Prov 6:16: six things YHWH hates, seven are an abomination; Amos 1:3: for three transgressions, for four; Eccl 11:2). The pattern implies completeness-beyond-completeness: no matter how many troubles come, YHWH delivers. The sevenfold structure frames vv.20-22 as illustrations. The verse is genuine pastoral comfort but employs a formula that, as the epilogue shows, Job's friends applied mechanically rather than discerningly.
20: ḇərāʿāḇ pāḏəḵā mimmāweṯ ûḇamilḥāmāh mîyəḏê-ḥāreḇ: 'In famine he will redeem you from death, and in war from the power of the sword.' The verb pāḏāh (redeem, ransom) is the commercial/legal term for buying someone out of slavery or death-obligation. It is used in the OT for YHWH's ransoming Israel from Egypt (Deut 7:8; 15:15) and will be used by Job himself in the hope that his gōʾēl (kinsman-redeemer, 19:25) will vindicate him. The pairing of famine and sword echoes the classic triad of divine judgment (sword, famine, pestilence, cf. Jer 14:12; Ezek 5:12) — but here reversed into forms of divine deliverance.
21: bəšōṭ lāšôn tiṣṣāp̄ēn wəlōʾ-ṯîrāʾ mîššōḏ kî yāḇôʾ: 'You shall be hidden from the lash of the tongue, and shall not fear destruction when it comes.' Šôṭ lāšôn (scourge/lash of the tongue) is a vivid hapax for the destructive power of slander or false accusation. Šōḏ (destruction, devastation) is a standard prophetic term for sudden ruin (Isa 13:6; Joel 1:15; Zeph 1:15, where it occurs in the formula šōḏ miššadday, destruction from the Almighty). The promise of protection from tongue-violence is especially resonant in Job's situation: in the dialogues, the friends' words themselves become a kind of scourging.
22: ləšōḏ ûlərāʿāḇ tiśḥāq ûmijjayaṯ hāʾāreṣ ʾal-ṯîrāʾ: 'At destruction and famine you shall laugh, and shall not fear the beasts of the earth.' The image of laughing at destruction (śāḥaq) echoes the leviathan's response to Job's weapons in the whirlwind speech (41:29: 'He laughs at the rattle of the javelin'). The restoration of the righteous reverses the threat: what once brought terror becomes occasion for confident laughter. The promise of peace with nature's beasts (v.23) continues the Edenic-restoration imagery that appears throughout the OT in descriptions of eschatological restoration (Isa 11:6-9; 65:25).
23: kî ʿim-ʾaḇnê haśśāḏeh bəriṯeḵā ûḥayyaṯ haśśāḏeh hišlěmāh-lāḵ: 'For you shall be in league with the stones of the field, and the beasts of the field shall be at peace with you.' The covenant (bərît) with stones is unusual and debated — it may mean either a covenant of safety with the land itself (stones/rocks as obstacles removed) or an alliance metaphor (the land's hostility is dissolved). The šālôm-peace with wild animals evokes Hosea 2:18's eschatological covenant: 'I will make for them a covenant on that day with the beasts of the field, the birds of the heavens, and the creeping things of the ground.' The restoration language anticipates the new creation's peace but Eliphaz applies it pragmatically as a condition of Job's repentance-and-restoration.
24: wəyāḏaʿtā kî-šālôm ʾohŏleḵā ûp̄āqaḏtā nāweḵā wəlōʾ ṯeḥeṭāʾ: 'You shall know that your tent is at peace, and you shall inspect your fold and miss nothing.' Šālôm (wholeness, peace, well-being) of the tent is the culminating domestic image of restoration — the home as secure refuge. The verb pāqaḏ (to visit, inspect, take inventory) when applied to flocks means to count and find everything present. Nāweh (dwelling, fold, pasture) is the same word used in v.3 of the fool whose dwelling was cursed — the contrast is deliberate: repent, and your nāweh will be inspected with satisfaction rather than cursed.
25: wəyāḏaʿtā kî-raḇ zarʿeḵā ûṣeʾěṣāʾeḵā kəʿēśeḇ hāʾāreṣ: 'You shall know also that your offspring shall be many, and your descendants as the grass of the earth.' The restoration of children and descendants completes the picture: the specific losses of Job's prologue (children killed, 1:18-19) are directly addressed. The comparison to grass (ʿēśeḇ hāʾāreṣ) echoes the Abrahamic blessing language — offspring as innumerable as stars/sand/dust (Gen 13:16; 15:5; 22:17). Eliphaz presents the covenant blessing pattern as the natural result of submission to discipline. The epilogue does in fact restore Job's children (42:13-16), but not through Eliphaz's mechanism of formulaic repentance.
26: tāḇôʾ ḇeqeleḥ ʾeleyqāḇer kiʿălôṯ gāḏîš bəʿittô: 'You shall come to your grave in ripe old age, like a sheaf gathered up in its season.' Qeleḥ (ripe age, full vigor) is a hapax legomenon describing the peak of physical maturity — death at the right time, not prematurely. The agricultural simile (gāḏîš, a harvest stack/shock of grain) gathered bəʿittô (in its season/time) is the ideal death: the full-term harvest, not green grain cut down untimely. This directly addresses Job's earlier lament about dying in misery (3:11: 'Why did I not die at birth?'). The ideal death in wisdom literature is the śeḇaʿ yāmîm (full of days, Gen 35:29; Job 42:17) — Job's restoration will fulfill exactly this promise.
27: hinnēh-zōʾṯ ḥăqarnûhā kēn-hîʾ šəmaʿāh wəʾattāh ḏaʿ-lāḵ: 'Behold, this we have searched out; it is true. Hear, and know it for your good.' Eliphaz closes his speech with an appeal to communal investigation: ḥāqar (to search out, investigate) is the wisdom vocabulary for careful inquiry (Job 8:8; Prov 25:2; Eccl 1:13). The plural 'we have searched' invokes the authority of the wisdom tradition — these are not Eliphaz's personal opinions but the collected findings of the sages. The final imperative ḏaʿ-lāḵ (know it for yourself, take it to heart) is pastoral but also condescending: Eliphaz presents himself as transmitting received wisdom to an ignorant sufferer. YHWH's verdict in 42:7 will demolish this claim to certainty.
